skill.name: shell-scripting
skill.description: Best practices and reusable patterns for writing Bash and Zsh scripts, including CLI structure, error handling, input handling, and text processing.
skill.summary: Write safer Bash and Zsh automation scripts with practical patterns and examples.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell command, substitution, filesystem, weak crypto, and password-file patterns, but these occur inside Markdown examples for a shell scripting guide. No prompt injection, network access, credential exfiltration, or executable payload was found. The only retained concern is that one example reads /etc/passwd, which is educational but should be treated as sensitive system-file access.

low_findings.title: System Account File Used In Example
low_findings.description: The guide includes an awk example that reads /etc/passwd. This appears to be a common delimiter example, not malicious code, but it normalizes reading a sensitive system account file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact path /etc/passwd is present in the documented command, so the sensitive-file pattern is real. Confidence is not higher because the context is an educational text-processing example rather than an executable payload.

low_findings.title: Benign Shell Examples Trigger Command Execution Patterns
low_findings.description: The static analyzer detected many command execution and command substitution patterns. They are contained in Markdown code fences and best-practice notes for a shell scripting guide, with no evidence of automatic execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious commands appear inside fenced Markdown examples and explanatory bullet lists. I found no executable script file, install hook, network call, or hidden command runner.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography matches point to shell-related text such as the Bash and Zsh description, a script description comment, and exit-code guidance. No hashing or encryption implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic APIs or algorithm choices. They are natural-language shell documentation, so the weak crypto classification is unsupported.

low_findings.title: Certificate Or Key Static Match Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The certificate or key-file match points to a jq example selecting a JSON field named key. It does not reference private keys, certificates, or credential files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line uses the literal JSON property name key in a jq tutorial example. There is no evidence of file access to certificate material or secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Documentation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches Bash and Zsh syntax with command substitutions, file tests, redirects, find, grep, jq, awk, and sed. These are expected for the domain, but users should review generated scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples in a Markdown guide and match the skill purpose. They can be risky if copied into production without review, but they are not hidden execution logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: System File Path Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The /etc/passwd path appears in an awk text-processing example. This is not evidence of malicious intent, but it is a sensitive path and should be treated cautiously.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive system-file path is directly present. The broader context is instructional and no exfiltration or persistence behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Shell scripts often fail because quoting, errors, and inputs are handled inconsistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Bash and Zsh patterns for safer automation.
actual_capabilities: Provides a reusable Bash script template with strict mode, argument parsing, logging, and help output.
Explains shell error handling with set options, traps, cleanup functions, and explicit status checks.
Shows Bash and Zsh variable expansion, arrays, associative arrays, loops, and conditionals.
Covers input, output, redirection, file checks, command checks, and safe line reading patterns.
Includes text-processing examples using find, grep, jq, awk, sed, and tee.
Lists practical do and do-not guidance for quoting, eval, ShellCheck, paths, and modular scripts.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not run or verify scripts automatically.
Examples are general patterns and must be adapted to each operating system and shell version.
It does not provide a full testing framework for shell scripts.
Some examples use placeholder commands that require user review before execution.

use_cases.title: Create Deployment Helpers
use_cases.description: Build shell scripts that check dependencies, handle errors, and report failures clearly during release tasks.

use_cases.title: Automate Local Workflows
use_cases.description: Turn repeated file, logging, and text-processing tasks into maintainable Bash or Zsh scripts.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Scripts
use_cases.description: Improve quoting, argument parsing, cleanup, and unsafe command usage in legacy shell scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the shell-scripting skill to draft a Bash script for this task: [describe task]. Include strict mode, help text, logging, and safe argument handling.

prompt_templates.title: Add Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the shell-scripting skill to improve this script with safer error handling, cleanup traps, dependency checks, and clear failure messages: [paste script].

prompt_templates.title: Build a CLI Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the shell-scripting skill to design a Bash CLI tool for [workflow]. Include options, validation, dry-run behavior, output rules, and examples.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the shell-scripting skill to audit this shell script for quoting bugs, unsafe eval use, path handling, missing traps, and ShellCheck issues: [paste script].

output_examples.input: Create a script that processes log files and supports dry-run mode.
output_examples.output: A structured script plan with strict mode, argument parsing, dependency checks, logging, dry-run handling, and safe file iteration guidance.

output_examples.input: Review a script that fails when file names contain spaces.
output_examples.output: A focused review explaining where quoting is missing, how to read lines safely, and how to test paths with spaces.

output_examples.input: Design a small CLI for batch renaming files.
output_examples.output: A CLI outline with option names, validation steps, confirmation behavior, dry-run output, and safety checks before renaming files.

best_practices: Ask the assistant to include strict mode, quoting, cleanup, and dependency checks in every generated script.
Run ShellCheck and test generated scripts with paths that include spaces before production use.
Use dry-run behavior for scripts that modify files, services, or deployment state.
anti_patterns: Do not execute generated shell commands before reviewing their file, network, and permission effects.
Do not use eval, unquoted variables, or parsed ls output in scripts that handle untrusted input.
Do not assume Linux-only paths or commands are available on macOS, containers, or minimal systems.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for writing scripts. Any generated command must be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Can it help with both Bash and Zsh?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is centered on Bash and Zsh patterns, with emphasis on portable shell practices where possible.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production automation?
faq.answer: It can help draft production scripts, but scripts still need review, testing, ShellCheck, and environment-specific validation.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It warns about unsafe eval use, missing quotes, unchecked paths, ignored warnings, and fragile parsing patterns.

faq.question: Can it debug an existing script?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the script and the failure symptoms, then ask for error handling, quoting, and control-flow review.

faq.question: What tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It includes examples with common shell tools such as find, grep, jq, awk, sed, tee, and ShellCheck guidance.
